# Diesel ORM Best Practices

## Overview
Diesel is a compile-time safe ORM and query builder for Rust. Use it for projects that require strong static guarantees on database interactions — the DSL is verified against your schema at compile time, eliminating an entire class of runtime SQL errors. Avoid it when you need dynamic runtime query construction or ad-hoc SQL that doesn't fit the type-safe DSL; reach for `sqlx` with raw SQL instead.

## Architecture Patterns

### Schema and model definitions
```rust
// schema.rs — auto-generated by `diesel print-schema`, commit to VCS
diesel::table! {
    users (id) {
        id -> Uuid,
        email -> Text,
        created_at -> Timestamptz,
    }
}

// models.rs
#[derive(Queryable, Selectable, Identifiable, Debug)]
#[diesel(table_name = crate::schema::users)]
pub struct User {
    pub id: Uuid,
    pub email: String,
    pub created_at: DateTime<Utc>,
}

#[derive(Insertable)]
#[diesel(table_name = crate::schema::users)]
pub struct NewUser<'a> {
    pub id: Uuid,
    pub email: &'a str,
}
```

### Repository pattern
```rust
pub struct UserRepository {
    pool: deadpool_diesel::postgres::Pool,
}

impl UserRepository {
    pub async fn find_by_id(&self, user_id: Uuid) -> Result<Option<User>> {
        let conn = self.pool.get().await?;
        conn.interact(move |conn| {
            users::table.find(user_id).first::<User>(conn).optional()
        })
        .await?
        .map_err(Into::into)
    }

    pub async fn insert(&self, new_user: NewUser<'_>) -> Result<User> {
        let conn = self.pool.get().await?;
        conn.interact(move |conn| {
            diesel::insert_into(users::table)
                .values(&new_user)
                .get_result::<User>(conn)
        })
        .await?
        .map_err(Into::into)
    }
}
```

### Embedding migrations
```rust
// Run at startup — ensures schema is always up to date
pub fn run_migrations(conn: &mut PgConnection) -> Result<()> {
    conn.run_pending_migrations(MIGRATIONS)?;
    Ok(())
}

// MIGRATIONS is generated by embed_migrations!() in build.rs or via the macro
```

## Configuration

```toml
# Cargo.toml
[dependencies]
diesel = { version = "2", features = ["postgres", "uuid", "chrono"] }
deadpool-diesel = { version = "0.5", features = ["postgres"] }
diesel_migrations = "2"

[build-dependencies]
# diesel CLI used externally: cargo install diesel_cli --no-default-features --features postgres
```

```toml
# diesel.toml — controls print-schema behavior
[print_schema]
file = "src/schema.rs"
custom_type_derives = ["diesel::query_builder::QueryId", "Clone"]
```

## Performance

- Use `deadpool-diesel` (or `r2d2`) for connection pooling; never create ad-hoc connections.
- Batch inserts with `insert_into(...).values(slice)` — a single round-trip for many rows.
- Use `.select(specific_columns)` with `Selectable` structs to avoid `SELECT *` on wide tables.
- Prefer `.filter()` chains over fetching and filtering in Rust — push predicates to the DB.
- Use `.limit()` and `.offset()` with explicit ordering for pagination; keyset pagination is more efficient for large tables.

```rust
// Batch insert
diesel::insert_into(events::table)
    .values(&new_events)   // &Vec<NewEvent>
    .execute(conn)?;

// Select subset of columns
users::table
    .select(UserSummary::as_select())
    .load::<UserSummary>(conn)?;
```

## Security

- Never use `sql()` or `diesel::dsl::sql` with user-supplied data — parameterize via `.filter(col.eq(value))` instead.
- Use UUIDs as primary keys to prevent enumeration attacks via sequential IDs.
- Apply row-level filters (tenant_id, owner_id) as Diesel filters, not application-level post-filtering.
- Store connection strings in environment variables or a secrets manager; never in source code.

## Testing

```rust
#[cfg(test)]
mod tests {
    use diesel::Connection;
    use testcontainers::clients::Cli;
    use testcontainers_modules::postgres::Postgres;

    fn establish_test_conn() -> PgConnection {
        // Use a Testcontainer for isolated DB state
        let docker = Cli::default();
        let node = docker.run(Postgres::default());
        let url = format!("postgres://postgres@127.0.0.1:{}/postgres",
                          node.get_host_port_ipv4(5432));
        PgConnection::establish(&url).unwrap()
    }

    #[test]
    fn insert_and_find_user() {
        let mut conn = establish_test_conn();
        conn.test_transaction::<_, diesel::result::Error, _>(|conn| {
            let inserted = diesel::insert_into(users::table)
                .values(NewUser { id: Uuid::new_v4(), email: "a@b.com" })
                .get_result::<User>(conn)?;
            let found = users::table.find(inserted.id).first::<User>(conn)?;
            assert_eq!(found.email, "a@b.com");
            Ok(())
        });
    }
}
```

## Dos
- Commit `schema.rs` to version control — it is the source of truth for the type-safe DSL.
- Use `deadpool-diesel` for async runtimes; wrap blocking Diesel calls in `.interact()`.
- Use `embed_migrations!()` to bundle migrations into the binary for zero-dependency deployment.
- Derive `Selectable` and use `.select(Model::as_select())` to make queries explicit.
- Use `conn.test_transaction()` in tests to roll back after each case.

## Don'ts
- Don't call `diesel::sql_query()` with user input — it bypasses compile-time safety.
- Don't skip regenerating `schema.rs` after a migration — stale schema causes compile errors.
- Don't use synchronous Diesel directly in async code without `.interact()` or `spawn_blocking`.
- Don't store `PgConnection` directly; always use a pool.
- Don't use `EAGER` fetch equivalents; Diesel has no lazy loading — load associations explicitly.
